summary: 10 items surfaced from HN comment threads.

items.name: WhatCable
items.source_story_title: Show HN: WhatCable, a tiny menu bar app for inspecting USB-C cables
items.summary: Tiny macOS utility that reads what USB-C cables can actually do.
items.why_included: HN commenters immediately wanted ports and alternate packaging, showing real demand for the utility.
items.evidence: The thread quickly turned into requests for CLI, KDE/GNOME, Homebrew, MacPorts, and regular-app modes.

items.name: AI CAD Harness
items.summary: CAD agent that edits existing Fusion and Onshape models.
items.why_included: A rare in-CAD AI launch that generated genuine workflow discussion.
items.evidence: Comments focused on CAD-as-code, feature-tree edits, and beta integrations for Fusion and Onshape.

items.name: My Private GitHub on Postgres
items.source_story_title: Show HN: My Private GitHub on Postgres
items.summary: Postgres-backed Git server designed around database backups.
items.why_included: Interesting storage-model twist that prompted real self-hosting comparisons.
items.evidence: Commenters liked the backup integration and kept comparing it to Fossil and GitLab/Forgejo/Gitea.

items.name: whohas
items.source_story_title: whohas – Command-line utility for cross-distro, cross-repository package search
items.summary: Cross-distro package-search CLI that reduces distro-hopping.
items.why_included: Commenters called it a boring but useful tool and pointed to adjacent search resources.
items.evidence: The thread cited Repology, pkgs.org, and nix-locate as alternatives or complements.

items.name: Fossil
items.source_story_title: Show HN: My Private GitHub on Postgres
items.summary: All-in-one SCM with hosting, tracking, wiki, and chat.
items.why_included: Repeatedly recommended as the cleaner self-hosted Git alternative.
items.evidence: Commenters said “Fossil really has it all” and “Just use Fossil at this point.”

items.name: Homebrew on Linux
items.source_story_title: whohas – Command-line utility for cross-distro, cross-repository package search
items.summary: Package manager option people said now fits everyday Linux workflows.
items.why_included: A practical distro-neutral fallback that changed one commenter’s setup.
items.evidence: One comment said switching to Homebrew for Linux and Flatpak made distro choice mostly about desktop environment.

items.name: Repology
items.source_story_title: whohas – Command-line utility for cross-distro, cross-repository package search
items.summary: Package metadata index with an hourly-updated API.
items.why_included: Mentioned as the structured package-search alternative in the whohas thread.
items.evidence: A commenter said Repology provides an API, though unstable.

items.name: pkgs.org
items.source_story_title: whohas – Command-line utility for cross-distro, cross-repository package search
items.summary: Cross-distro package lookup site with broad repo coverage.
items.why_included: Named alongside Repology as a useful fallback package search resource.
items.evidence: The whohas thread explicitly linked pkgs.org right after discussing Repology.

items.name: nix-index
items.source_story_title: whohas – Command-line utility for cross-distro, cross-repository package search
items.summary: Nix package locator behind the nix-locate command.
items.why_included: Suggested as the CLI answer when package names vary across distros.
items.evidence: A commenter pointed to `nix-locate -r 'bin/foo$'` as the command-line alternative.

items.name: NumWorks
items.summary: Graphing calculator users praised as cheaper and better than TI.
items.why_included: A strong alternative signal in the Ti-84 Evo thread.
items.evidence: Multiple comments said NumWorks is similar but more reasonably priced, and one said it is much better.
